# The Fork

> A fork is a single move in which one piece (or pawn) attacks two or more enemy targets at the same time, so the opponent cannot save them all.

**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
**Prerequisites:** [Tactics fundamentals](tactics-fundamentals.md), [Counting and undefended pieces](loose-pieces.md)

## The idea

A fork exploits the simplest truth in chess: a move can only do one thing at a time. If you attack two enemy pieces with one of yours, your opponent gets one move to respond. They move or defend one target — you take the other.

Every piece can fork. The knight is the most famous forker because it jumps, so it can hit two pieces that are far apart and even pieces defending each other. But pawns, bishops, rooks, queens and even the king all fork constantly. The strongest forks hit the **king** (a check that *must* be answered) plus a valuable, undefended piece. That is why the "royal fork" (king + queen) and the "family fork" (king + queen + rook, sometimes more) win so much material.

## Why it matters

Forks are the highest-frequency tactic below master level. The vast majority of decisive games at 1500–2000 turn on a fork that one side missed — either failing to spot their own or walking into the opponent's. Learning to see knight-distance relationships and loose-piece geometry instantly will win you more rating points than any opening study. A fork that nets a queen for a knight ends the game on the spot.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . k . r
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . N . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . K . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White's knight on f6 forks the black king on f8 and the rook on h8.*
**FEN:** 5k1r/8/5N2/8/8/8/8/5K2 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/5k1r/8/5N2/8/8/8/8/5K2_w_-_-_0_1)

Here **1.Nxh7+? is wrong** — the knight is already on f6. The fork already exists: the knight on f6 attacks **h7? no — it attacks g8, h7, e8, d7, d5, e4, g4, h5**. The relevant squares are **g8 and h7**, but the targets are the king (f8) and rook (h8). Reset cleanly: from f6 the knight gives **no check** to a king on f8. The instructive version is the knight on **g6**:

```
8 . . . . . k . r
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . N .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . K . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Knight on g6 checks the king on f8 and simultaneously attacks the rook on h8 — a textbook king+rook fork.*
**FEN:** 5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2_w_-_-_0_1)

The knight on g6 attacks **f8 (check) and h8**. After **1...Ke8** (or any king move) **2.Nxh8** wins the rook clean. Note both forked squares, f8 and h8, are a knight's-move from g6. The forking piece survives because the king must escape check first.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Royal fork:** knight (usually) checks the king and attacks the queen. Wins the queen outright.
- **Family fork:** one knight check hits king + queen + rook (the "family" of major pieces), often from a square like e7, f7, c7 or d6 in the opponent's camp.
- **Pawn fork:** a pawn pushes to attack two pieces on the two diagonally-forward squares; cheap and devastating because the attacker is worth one point.
- **Bishop fork:** the bishop forks two pieces on the same diagonal color, frequently king + rook on opposite corners.
- **Rook fork:** a rook on a rank or file skewers two undefended pieces, e.g. king on the 8th and a rook stranded on the same rank.
- **Queen fork:** the queen's range makes it the most flexible forker; common as a check plus an attack on a loose piece.
- **Absolute fork:** one target is the king (a check) — the most forcing, because the response is not optional.
- **Relative fork:** neither target is the king; the opponent may have a counter-attacking defense.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[My move to play] --> B[List all enemy loose or high-value pieces]
  B --> C{Two targets a knight move apart}
  C -->|yes| D[Check for knight fork]
  C -->|no| E{Two targets on one line or diagonal}
  E -->|yes| F[Check rook bishop or queen fork]
  E -->|no| G{Pawn push hits two pieces}
  G -->|yes| H[Play the pawn fork]
  D --> I{Is the forking square safe}
  F --> I
  I -->|yes| J[Execute and win material]
  I -->|no| K[Look for a different tactic]
```

Train your eye to flag the enemy **king and queen first**, then any **undefended** piece, then ask whether one of your pieces can reach a square that touches two of them.

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — Pawn fork

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . n . b . . .
3 . . . P . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White's pawn on d3 advances to d4, attacking the knight on c4 and the bishop on e4 at once.*
**FEN:** 7k/8/8/8/2n1b3/3P4/8/7K w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/8/8/8/2n1b3/3P4/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.d4!** forks the knight and the bishop. Black saves one piece, e.g. **1...Bd5**, and White plays **2.dxc4** winning a clean piece. A one-point pawn nets a three-point minor.

### Example 2 — Royal fork (knight checks king, wins queen)

```
8 . . . . k . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . q . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . N . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*The knight on e4 jumps to d6, checking the king on e8 and attacking the queen on d6's... no — landing on d6 hits e8 and forks the queen.*
**FEN:** 4k3/8/3q4/8/4N3/8/8/7K w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/4k3/8/3q4/8/4N3/8/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)

Here the queen sits on d6. The knight needs a square attacking both **e8 (king)** and **d6 (queen)** — that square is **c7**? c7 attacks e8 and a8 and a6 and b5 and d5 and e6 — not d6. The clean royal-fork square that hits e8 and d6 is **f7? no.** The square attacking both e8 and d6 is **c7 and f7 fail; the correct fork square is from a knight reaching both, e.g. the knight already on e4 plays 1.Nxd6+** — it simply **captures the queen with check**, since e4–d6 is a knight move and d6 gives check to e8 along no line — d6 knight checks e8. So **1.Nxd6+!** wins the queen with check immediately; after the king moves, White is up a full queen.

## Common mistakes

- Forking onto a square the enemy can simply capture (the forking piece is hanging).
- Forking two pieces that **defend each other** with a rook/bishop/queen — the opponent recaptures and you only trade.
- Forgetting that a non-check (relative) fork lets the opponent counter with their own check or threat.
- Missing forks **against you**: always check enemy knight landing squares before you move.
- Choosing the lower-value target when a tempo would let you grab the higher one with check.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle themes:** [Fork](https://lichess.org/training/fork), plus the related [Knight endgame](https://lichess.org/training/knightEndgame) and [Hanging piece](https://lichess.org/training/hangingPiece) themes. Do **20 fork puzzles per day** for two weeks.
- **Knight-vision drill:** place a lone knight on a board and, for 5 minutes daily, name every square it attacks from each starting square until it is instant.
- **Books:** *Chess Tactics for Students* (John Bain) — the fork chapters; *1001 Chess Exercises for Beginners* (Frank Erwich); *Understanding Chess Tactics* (Weteschnik) for the deeper "loose pieces drop off" principle.
- **Blitz review:** after each blitz game, scan for one fork you or your opponent missed. Log 1 per game.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win the rook.
2. **FEN:** `7k/8/8/8/2n1b3/3P4/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win a piece with a pawn.
3. **FEN:** `4k3/8/3q4/8/4N3/8/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win the queen.
4. **FEN:** `6k1/8/8/3n4/8/8/5PPP/6K1 b - - 0 1` — Black to move. Fork the king and a pawn... find the best knight check.
5. **FEN:** `4k3/8/8/8/8/8/4r3/R3K3 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Is there a fork? Decide and play.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Nxh8** is too slow if the king defends; instead the knight already checks: after the forced **1...K-moves** play **2.Nxh8** winning the rook. The g6-knight forks f8 (king) and h8 (rook).
2. **1.d4!** forks the knight on c4 and bishop on e4. Whatever Black saves, **2.dxc4** or **2.dxe... (capture the other)** wins a clean minor piece.
3. **1.Nxd6+!** captures the queen with check (e4–d6 is a knight move; d6 checks the king on e8). White is up a queen.
4. **1...Nf4** does nothing; the cleanest is **1...Ne3**, attacking nothing of value here, so the honest answer is **there is no winning fork** — the pawns on f2/g2/h2 sit out of knight range from d5. The lesson: verify the geometry before committing. (From d5 the knight reaches e3, c3, b4, b6, c7, e7, f6, f4 — none hit f2/g2/h2.)
5. **No fork — it is a pin/standoff.** White's rook on a1 and king on e1 are on the first rank with Black's rook on e2. Best is **1.Rxa-... no a-file is empty**; simply **1.Kxe2** is illegal if defended. The instructive point: not every two-target alignment is a fork — here the black rook on e2 is defended by the king on e8 along the e-file, so grabbing it must be checked first.

</details>

## Related

- [Double attack](double-attack.md) — the family the fork belongs to.
- [Discovered attack](discovered-attack.md) — two attacks from two pieces, not one.
- [Skewer](skewer.md) and [Pin](pin.md) — line tactics often confused with forks.
- [Deflection](deflection.md) and [Loose pieces](loose-pieces.md) — what creates forkable targets.

Concept tags: [[fork]] [[double-attack]] [[knight-fork]] [[royal-fork]] [[family-fork]] [[pawn-fork]]

A fork exploits the simplest truth in chess: a move can only do one thing at a time. If you attack two enemy pieces with one move, your opponent gets a single reply. They rescue one target — you take the other.

That is exactly why the "royal fork" (king + queen) and the "family fork" (king + queen + rook) win so much material: the king move is forced, and the second target falls for free.

Training your eye to see knight-distance relationships and loose-piece geometry instantly will win you more rating points than almost any opening study. ```

```

  a b c d e f g h
```
*Knight on c7 checks the king on e8 and attacks the queen on f6 — no; here it forks the king and a target on a knight's reach. See the moves below.*
**FEN:** 4k3/2N5/5q2/8/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/4k3/2N5/5q2/8/8/8/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)

- **Relative fork:** neither target is the king, so the defender may have a counter-threat.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[My move to play] --> B[List enemy loose or high value pieces]

```

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — Pawn fork

```

```

**FEN:** 7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K w - - 0 1

# The Fork

> A fork is a single move in which one piece or pawn attacks two or more enemy targets at the same time, so the opponent cannot save them all.

**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
**Prerequisites:** [Tactics fundamentals](tactics-fundamentals.md), [Loose pieces](loose-pieces.md)

## The idea

A fork exploits the simplest truth in chess: a move can only do one thing at a time. If you attack two enemy pieces with one move, your opponent gets a single reply. They rescue one target — you take the other.

Every piece can fork. The knight is the most famous forker because it jumps, so it can hit two pieces that are far apart and even pieces that defend each other. But pawns, bishops, rooks, queens, and even the king fork constantly. The strongest forks hit the **king** (a check that *must* be answered) plus a valuable, undefended piece. That is exactly why the **royal fork** (king + queen) and the **family fork** (king + queen + rook) win so much material: the king move is forced, and the second target falls for free.

## Why it matters

Forks are the highest-frequency winning tactic below master level. Most decisive games at 1500–2000 turn on a fork that one side missed — failing to spot their own, or walking into the opponent's. Training your eye to read knight-distance relationships and loose-piece geometry instantly will earn you more rating points than almost any opening study. A single royal fork that nets a queen for a knight ends the game on the spot.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . k . r
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . N .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . K . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Knight on g6 checks the king on f8 and simultaneously attacks the rook on h8 — a textbook king + rook fork.*
**FEN:** 5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2_w_-_-_0_1)

The knight on g6 attacks **f8 (check)** and **h8** — both a knight's-move away. Black must answer the check first, for example **1...Ke7**, and then **2.Nxh8** wins the rook clean. The knight survives precisely because the king has to step out of check before it can defend h8.

```

7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . q . . .
5 . N . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Knight on b5 is one hop from the royal fork: 1.Nc7+ forks the king on e8 and the queen on e6.*
**FEN:** 4k3/8/4q3/1N6/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/4k3/8/4q3/1N6/8/8/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)

White plays **1.Nc7+!** From c7 the knight attacks **e8 (check)** and **e6 (queen)** — verify the geometry: c7 reaches a6, a8, b5, d5, e6, e8. The king must move out of check, e.g. **1...Kd8**, and then **2.Nxe6+** wins the queen. This is the royal fork in its purest form: a one-move knight check that also hits the queen.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Royal fork:** a knight checks the king and attacks the queen at once. Wins the queen outright.
- **Family fork:** one knight check hits king + queen + rook, typically landing on e7, f7, c7, or d6 deep in the enemy camp.
- **Pawn fork:** a pawn advances to attack the two squares diagonally in front of it; cheap and brutal because the attacker is worth one point.
- **Bishop fork:** the bishop hits two pieces on the same colored diagonal, often king + rook in opposite corners.
- **Rook fork:** a rook on a rank or file attacks two undefended pieces at once.
- **Queen fork:** the queen's range makes it the most flexible forker — usually a check plus a hit on a loose piece.
- **Absolute fork:** one target is the king (a check) — the most forcing, since the reply is not optional.
- **Relative fork:** neither target is the king, so the defender may have a counter-threat.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[My move to play] --> B[List enemy loose or high value pieces]
  B --> C{Two targets a knight move apart}
  C -->|yes| D[Check a knight fork]
  C -->|no| E{Two targets on one line or diagonal}
  E -->|yes| F[Check rook bishop or queen fork]
  E -->|no| G{A pawn push hits two pieces}
  G -->|yes| H[Play the pawn fork]
  D --> I{Is the landing square safe}
  F --> I
  I -->|yes| J[Execute and win material]
  I -->|no| K[Look for another tactic]
```

Flag the enemy **king and queen first**, then any **undefended** piece, then ask whether one of your pieces can reach a square that touches two of them.

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — Pawn fork

```

4 . . . . . . . .

2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White's pawn on d3 pushes to d4, attacking the knight on c5 and the bishop on e5 at the same time.*
**FEN:** 7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.d4!** A pawn on d4 attacks **c5** and **e5** — both black minors at once. Black saves one, say **1...Bd6**, and White plays **2.dxc5** winning a clean piece. A one-point pawn has netted a three-point minor.

### Example 2 — Family fork

```

7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . q . . . .
5 . . . . . N . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White's knight on f5 lands on d6, checking the king on e8 and attacking both the queen on d6's... see the move: 1.Nd6+ is impossible as d6 is the landing square — the knight forks from there.*
**FEN:** 4k2r/8/3q4/5N2/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/4k2r/8/3q4/5N2/8/8/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)

The knight on f5 cannot reach a square forking all three (f5 reaches d4, d6, e3, e7, g3, g7, h4, h6). But **1.Ne7!** lands on e7, attacking **c8, c6, d5, f5, g6, g8** — none of these is the king or rook here. The clean family fork in this exact setup is **1.Nxd6+** capturing the queen *with check* (f5→d6, and d6 attacks e8): Black recaptures or moves the king, and White has won the queen. The "family" version (also hitting the rook) appears in Exercise 3, where the knight lands on a square touching king, queen, and rook together.

## Common mistakes

- Forking onto a square where the enemy can simply capture the forking piece — it hangs.
- Using a rook, bishop, or queen to "fork" two pieces that **defend each other**: the opponent recaptures and you have only traded.
- Forgetting that a relative (non-check) fork lets the opponent reply with their own check or a bigger threat.
- Missing forks **against you** — always scan enemy knight landing squares before you move.
- Grabbing the lower-value target when a check would have let you win the higher one with tempo.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle themes:** the [Fork](https://lichess.org/training/fork) theme, plus [Hanging piece](https://lichess.org/training/hangingPiece). Do **20 fork puzzles per day** for two weeks.
- **Knight-vision drill:** place a lone knight on an empty board and, for 5 minutes daily, name every square it attacks from each square until it is instant.
- **Books:** *Chess Tactics for Students* (John Bain), the fork chapters; *1001 Chess Exercises for Beginners* (Frank Erwich); *Understanding Chess Tactics* (Weteschnik) for the deeper loose-pieces principle.
- **Game review:** after each game, find one fork you or your opponent missed. Log one per game.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win the rook.
2. **FEN:** `7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win a piece with a pawn.
3. **FEN:** `4k3/8/4q3/1N6/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Royal fork: win the queen with check.
4. **FEN:** `r3k3/8/8/8/8/8/8/4N1K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. From e1 the knight is two hops from a king + rook fork; find the move that begins it.
5. **FEN:** `4k3/8/8/8/7b/8/8/R3K2R w KQ - 0 1` — Black's bishop on h4 sits on the e1–h4 diagonal. White to move: is the rook on a1 actually attacked? Decide and play the best move.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. The knight on g6 already attacks **f8 (check)** and **h8**. After the forced **1...Ke7** (or Ke8/Kg8), **2.Nxh8** wins the rook clean.
2. **1.d4!** A pawn on d4 attacks **c5** and **e5**, forking the knight and bishop. Black saves one; **2.dxc5** (or dxe5) wins a clean minor piece.
3. **1.Nc7+!** From c7 the knight checks the king on e8 and attacks the queen on e6. After **1...Kd8** (or Kf8/Kd7), **2.Nxe6+** wins the queen.
4. **1.Nd3** heads for the fork: from d3 the knight eyes c5 and b4. The fastest is **1.Nc2** then **2.Na3** is too slow — instead **1.Nd3** intending **Nb4**, hitting a8? No. The reliable two-move route is **1.Nc2** then **Nb4**, since b4 attacks a6, c6, d5, d3, a2, c2 — not a8. The honest answer: from e1 there is **no quick king + rook fork** against Ke8/Ra8; the lesson is to verify reachability before committing. (A knight forks e8 from c7, d6, f6, or g7, and forks a8 from b6 or c7 — the shared square is **c7**, four hops from e1.)
5. **No — the rook on a1 is safe.** The bishop on h4 is a **dark-squared** bishop and a1 is a dark square, but the e1–h4 diagonal runs e1–f2–g3–h4; it does **not** pass through a1. Lesson: confirm a bishop's diagonal square by square before assuming what it attacks.

</details>

The king must move, e.g. **1...Kd8**, and then **2.Nxe6+** wins the queen. This is the royal fork in its purest form: a one-move knight check that also hits the queen.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Royal fork:** a knight checks the king and attacks the queen at once. Wins the queen outright.
- **Family fork:** one knight check hits king + queen + rook, typically landing on e7, f7, c7, or d6 deep in the enemy camp.
- **Pawn fork:** a pawn advances to attack the two squares diagonally in front of it; cheap and brutal because the attacker is worth one point.
- **Bishop fork:** the bishop hits two pieces on the same colored diagonal, often king + rook in opposite corners.
- **Rook fork:** a rook on a rank or file attacks two undefended pieces at once.
- **Queen fork:** the queen's range makes it the most flexible forker — usually a check plus a hit on a loose piece.
- **Absolute fork:** one target is the king (a check) — the most forcing, since the reply is not optional.
- **Relative fork:** neither target is the king, so the defender may have a counter-threat.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[My move to play] --> B[List enemy loose or high value pieces]
  B --> C{Two targets a knight move apart}
  C -->|yes| D[Check a knight fork]
  C -->|no| E{Two targets on one line or diagonal}
  E -->|yes| F[Check rook bishop or queen fork]
  E -->|no| G{A pawn push hits two pieces}
  G -->|yes| H[Play the pawn fork]
  D --> I{Is the landing square safe}
  F --> I
  I -->|yes| J[Execute and win material]
  I -->|no| K[Look for another tactic]
```

Flag the enemy **king and queen first**, then any **undefended** piece, then ask whether one of your pieces can reach a square that touches two of them.

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — Pawn fork

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . n . b . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . P . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White's pawn on d3 pushes to d4, attacking the knight on c5 and the bishop on e5 at the same time.*
**FEN:** 7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.d4!** A pawn on d4 attacks **c5** and **e5** — both black minors at once. Black saves one, say **1...Bd6**, and White plays **2.dxc5** winning a clean piece. A one-point pawn has netted a three-point minor.

### Example 2 — Family fork

```
8 . . r . . . k .
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . . N . . . . .
5 . . . . . q . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Knight on c6 jumps to e7, hitting the king on g8, the queen on f5, and the rook on c8 — the full family fork.*
**FEN:** 2r3k1/6pp/2N5/5q2/8/8/8/6K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/2r3k1/6pp/2N5/5q2/8/8/8/6K1_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.Ne7+!** From e7 the knight attacks **g8 (check)**, **f5 (queen)**, and **c8 (rook)** — its squares from e7 are c6, c8, d5, f5, g6, g8. The king must escape the check, e.g. **1...Kf8** or **1...Kh8**, and then **2.Nxf5** wins the queen (the rook was also forked, but the queen is the bigger prize). One knight check, three targets, game over.

## Common mistakes

- Forking onto a square where the enemy can simply capture the forking piece — it hangs.
- Using a rook, bishop, or queen to "fork" two pieces that **defend each other**: the opponent recaptures and you have only traded.
- Forgetting that a relative (non-check) fork lets the opponent reply with their own check or a bigger threat.
- Missing forks **against you** — always scan enemy knight landing squares before you move.
- Grabbing the lower-value target when a check would have let you win the higher one with tempo.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle themes:** the [Fork](https://lichess.org/training/fork) theme, plus [Hanging piece](https://lichess.org/training/hangingPiece). Do **20 fork puzzles per day** for two weeks.
- **Knight-vision drill:** place a lone knight on an empty board and, for 5 minutes daily, name every square it attacks from each square until it is instant.
- **Books:** *Chess Tactics for Students* (John Bain), the fork chapters; *1001 Chess Exercises for Beginners* (Frank Erwich); *Understanding Chess Tactics* (Weteschnik) for the deeper loose-pieces principle.
- **Game review:** after each game, find one fork you or your opponent missed. Log one per game.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win the rook.
2. **FEN:** `7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win a piece with a pawn.
3. **FEN:** `4k3/8/4q3/1N6/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Royal fork: win the queen with check.
4. **FEN:** `2r3k1/6pp/2N5/5q2/8/8/8/6K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Find the family fork.
5. **FEN:** `4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3 w - - 0 1` — White to move. A queen fork: win the knight with check.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. The knight on g6 already attacks **f8 (check)** and **h8**. After the forced **1...Ke7** (or Ke8/Kg8), **2.Nxh8** wins the rook clean.
2. **1.d4!** A pawn on d4 attacks **c5** and **e5**, forking the knight and bishop. Black saves one; **2.dxc5** (or dxe5) wins a clean minor piece.
3. **1.Nc7+!** checks the king on e8 and attacks the queen on e6. After **1...Kd8** (or Kf8/Kd7), **2.Nxe6+** wins the queen.
4. **1.Ne7+!** forks the king on g8, the queen on f5, and the rook on c8. After **1...Kf8** (or Kh8), **2.Nxf5** wins the queen.
5. **1.Qh5+!** The queen on h5 checks the king on e8 along the h5–e8 diagonal (h5–g6–f7–e8) and attacks the knight on b5 along the 5th rank. After the king moves, **2.Qxb5** wins the knight.

</details>
